summary:
- FDNY's 1965-1982 "Super Pumper" system was a single, high-volume pump on a semi-trailer that fed up to eight satellite trucks; the satellites were not self-powered pumps, so the system was not very flexible and was ultimately replaced by multiple smaller, more agile units.  
- The 1960s-70s technology of the Super Pumper and its satellites was a marvel of its time, but the logistical burden of moving the whole system and the need to lay thousands of feet of hose to multiple hydrants made it less practical than simply deploying several smaller, self-contained pumpers.  
- The FDNY Super Pumper could draw from eight hydrants at once, but the need for so much hose and the risk of supply failure led to its retirement in favor of smaller, more maneuverable units.  
- The 1960s concept of a single, giant pump and hose network has been superseded by the more flexible strategy of bringing several smaller, self-contained pumpers to the scene, each able to draw from one or two hydrants and feed its own attack lines.
